en.extract:
The rufous-lored kingfisher (Todiramphus winchelli), also known as Winchell's kingfisher, is a species of bird in the kingfisher family Alcedinidae. It is endemic to the Philippines, its natural habitat consists of tropical moist lowland forests. It is threatened by deforestation, and the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed it as a vulnerable species.


Description
The rufous-lored kingfisher is about 25 cm (9.8 in) long. The crown is blackish-blue, with cobalt-blue edges, and the lores and neck-collar are rufous. The upperparts are mostly blackish and dark blue, with a bright azure-blue rump. The underparts are white in the male, and buff in the female. The eyes are dark brown, the beak is black, and the legs are greyish. The juvenile bird is similar to the female, but with duller plumage. The subspecies are coloured different shades of blue. A black patch on the sides of the male's breast is conspicuous in subspecies nigrorum and concealed in others, and in nesydrionetes, the female has an orange breast forming a band between the whitish throat and belly.


Taxonomy

This species was described as Halcyon Wwnchelli by British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1877, using a specimen collected by American ornithologist Joseph Beal Steere. It was named after American geologist Newton Horace Winchell, as requested by Steere. Although two subspecies were later described, they were usually considered synonymous with the nominate before 1966. That year Kenneth C. Parkes studied a series of 45 specimens and recognised five subspecies, two of which were new. 


Subspecies
Five subspecies are recognized:

T. w. winchelli– Found on Basilan
T. w. nesydrionetes  – Found on Tablas Island, Romblon and Sibuyan Island; female has orange-buff neck sides and breastband, contrasting with whitish chin/throat and belly
T. w. nigrorum  – Found on Samar, Calicoan, Biliran, Leyte, Negros, Cebu, Siquijor and Bohol; bluish-black patch at side of breast, duller and blacker upperparts
T. w. mindanensis  – Found on Mindanao and Camiguin; similar to nigorum has blue areas strongly tinged purple, female  washed cinnamon or tawny-buff below
T. w. alfredi  – Found on Sulu Archipelago; male is buff below, like female of mindanensis, whereas female is even paler than that of mindanensis


Behaviour and ecology

This kingfisher often perches in the canopy and sometimes descends to lower perches and to the ground, probably to feed. One of its calls is an ascending series of harsh chup and chep notes, and another consists of three rising notes and then a long descending series chu chu chu chu. Loud squawking has also been heard. It batters its prey, which consists of large insects, spiders and small vertebrates. Little is known about its breeding. Nesting in a used arboreal termite nest has been recorded.


Habitat and Conservation status
This species is endemic to the Philippines, ranging from Samar and Tablas south to Mindanao, Basilan and the Sulu Archipelago. It appears to be locally common on some islands, but it is rare in other localities. It lives in forest below 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in elevation, being found in coastal lowlands and foothills. Its tolerance of degraded forest is uncertain.
The population size is estimated at 2,500–9,999 mature individuals, or 3,500–15,000 individuals in total. Forest clearance and illegal logging are causing habitat loss and a fast population decline, so the IUCN has assessed it as a vulnerable species. This species has been recorded in some protected areas, such as Mount Guiting-Guiting and Rajah Sikatuna Protected Landscape.


References


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet

''Halcyon winchelli'' Sharpe, 1877 }} The '''rufous-lored kingfisher''' ('''''Todiramphus winchelli)''''', also known as '''Winchell's kingfisher''', is a species of [[bird]] in the kingfisher family [[Alcedinidae]]. It is [[endemic]] to the [[Philippines]], its natural [[habitat]] consists of tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s. It is threatened by [[deforestation]], and the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) has assessed it as a vulnerable species. ==Description== The rufous-lored kingfisher is about {{convert|25|cm|abbr=on}} long.{{cite book |last1=Fry |first1=C. Hilary |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=U_XUBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA162 |title=Kingfishers, Bee-eaters and Rollers |last2=Fry |first2=Kathie |date=2010 |publisher=Bloomsbury |isbn=978-1-4081-3525-9 |pages=162–163 |orig-date=First published 1992}} The crown is blackish-blue, with cobalt-blue edges, and the [[lore (anatomy)|lores]] and neck-collar are rufous.{{cite book |last1=Kennedy |first1=Robert S. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fPDxk551AhkC&pg=PA203 |title=A Guide to the Birds of the Philippines |last2=Gonzales |first2=Pedro C. |last3=Dickinson |first3=Edward C. |last4=Miranda |first4=Hector C. Jr |last5=Fisher |first5=Timothy H. |date=2000 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-854668-9 |pages=203–204}} The upperparts are mostly blackish and dark blue, with a bright azure-blue rump. The underparts are white in the male, and buff in the female. The eyes are dark brown, the beak is black, and the legs are greyish. The [[juvenile (organism)|juvenile]] bird is similar to the female, but with duller plumage. The subspecies are coloured different shades of blue. A black patch on the sides of the male's breast is conspicuous in subspecies ''nigrorum'' and concealed in others, and in ''nesydrionetes'', the female has an orange breast forming a band between the whitish throat and belly. ==Taxonomy== [[File:Todiramphus winchelli 29276524.jpg|left|thumb|Male ssp. ''mindanensis'']] This species was described as ''Halcyon Wwnchelli'' by British ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1877, using a specimen collected by American ornithologist [[Joseph Beal Steere]].{{cite journal |last1=Sharpe |first1=R. B. |title=On the Birds collected by Professor J. B. Steere in the Philippine Archipelago |journal=Transactions of the Linnean Society of London |date=1877 |volume=1 |page=318 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28379496 |series=2}} It was named after American geologist [[Newton Horace Winchell]],{{cite book |last1=Jobling |first1=James A. |title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |date=2010 |publisher=Bloomsbury |isbn=978-1-4081-3326-2 |page=408 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-RfSBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA408}} as requested by Steere. Although two subspecies were later described, they were usually considered synonymous with the nominate before 1966. That year Kenneth C. Parkes studied a series of 45 specimens and recognised five subspecies, two of which were new.{{cite journal |last1=Parkes |first1=Kenneth C. |title=Geographic variation in Winchell's Kingfisher ''Halcyon winchelli'', of the Philippines |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=1966 |volume=86 |pages=82–86 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40815018}} === Subspecies === Five [[subspecies]] are recognized:{{cite web |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=F. |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=D. |title=Rollers, ground rollers, kingfishers |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/rollers/ |access-date=21 June 2018 |website=IOC World Bird List Version 8.1}} * ''T. w. winchelli''– Found on [[Basilan]] * ''T. w. nesydrionetes'' – Found on [[Tablas Island]], [[Romblon]] and [[Sibuyan Island]]; female has orange-buff neck sides and breastband, contrasting with whitish chin/throat and belly * ''T. w. nigrorum'' – Found on [[Samar]], [[Calicoan Island|Calicoan]], [[Biliran]], [[Leyte]], [[Negros]], [[Cebu]], [[Siquijor]] and [[Bohol]]; bluish-black patch at side of breast, duller and blacker upperparts * ''T. w. mindanensis'' – Found on [[Mindanao]] and [[Camiguin]]; similar to ''nigorum'' has blue areas strongly tinged purple, female  washed cinnamon or tawny-buff below * ''T. w. alfredi'' – Found on [[Sulu Archipelago]]; male is buff below, like female of ''mindanensis'', whereas female is even paler than that of ''mindanensis''{{Cite journal |last1=Woodall |first1=P. F. |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |last3=Bonan |first3=Arnau |date=2020 |title=Rufous-lored Kingfisher (Todiramphus winchelli), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/rulkin1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.rulkin1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }} ==Behaviour and ecology== [[File:Rufous-lored Kingfisher - Mindanao H8O1715 (15695535094).jpg|left|thumb]] This kingfisher often perches in the canopy and sometimes descends to lower perches and to the ground, probably to feed. One of its calls is an ascending series of harsh ''chup'' and ''chep'' notes, and another consists of three rising notes and then a long descending series ''chu chu chu chu''. Loud squawking has also been heard. It batters its prey, which consists of large insects, spiders and small vertebrates. Little is known about its breeding. Nesting in a used arboreal termite nest has been recorded. == Habitat and Conservation status == This species is endemic to the Philippines, ranging from [[Samar]] and [[Tablas Island|Tablas]] south to Mindanao, Basilan and the Sulu Archipelago. It appears to be locally common on some islands, but it is rare in other localities. It lives in forest below {{convert|1000|m|abbr=on}} in elevation, being found in coastal lowlands and foothills. Its tolerance of degraded forest is uncertain. The population size is estimated at 2,500–9,999 mature individuals, or 3,500–15,000 individuals in total. [[Forest clearance]] and [[illegal logging]] are causing [[habitat loss]] and a fast population decline, so the IUCN has assessed it as a [[vulnerable species]]. This species has been recorded in some protected areas, such as [[Mount Guiting-Guiting]] and [[Rajah Sikatuna Protected Landscape]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Wikispecies|Todiramphus winchelli}} *[http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/rufous-lored-kingfisher-todiramphus-winchelli BirdLife Species Factsheet] {{Halcyoninae}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1275204}} [[Category:Todiramphus|Winchell's kingfisher]] [[Category:Endemic birds of the Philippines]] [[Category:Birds described in 1877|Rufous-lored kingfisher]] [[Category:Taxonomy articles created by Polbot]]
